hook: Sharp railroad curve west of downtown where America's deadliest train wreck killed about 101 people in 1918.
facts: On 9 July 1918 two Nashville, Chattanooga & St. Louis Railway passenger trains collided head-on at Dutchman's Curve on a single-track section, killing roughly 101 people and injuring scores more.
It is generally regarded as the deadliest rail accident in United States history.
Many of the dead were African American laborers travelling to work at a wartime gunpowder plant.
A historical marker was erected in 2008 by the Historical Commission of Metropolitan Nashville and Davidson County, located on White Bridge Pike about 0.1 mile east of Post Road.
The curve sits near Richland Creek and is now flanked by the Richland Creek Greenway, west of downtown Nashville.
w: On a gentle railroad bend west of downtown, two passenger trains met head-on at speed on the morning of 9 July 1918, killing around 101 — the deadliest rail disaster in US history. Most of the dead were Black workers bound for the powder mills. A greenway marker is all that records it now. Stand on the curve and listen.

hook: Crocs co-founder's gated Bellevue front yard studded with life-size dinosaur and animal sculptures.
facts: The display fills the front grounds of a property at 7990 Poplar Creek Road in the Bellevue area of southwest Nashville (ZIP 37221).
It belongs to George Boedecker, a co-founder of the Crocs footwear company, who lives in Nashville.
The sculptures are predominantly dinosaurs but also include animals such as a lion, tiger and bear positioned near the gate.
Boedecker has said the pieces were made by an artist he met in Mexico, and that he assembled the display with his grandchildren and passing families in mind.
The grounds are private and gated; pieces sit well back on the large multi-acre lot, so it is view-only from the public road with no formal pull-off.
w: A Bellevue front yard erupts with giant dinosaurs, gorillas and other beasts — the private menagerie of George Boedecker, the co-founder of the Crocs shoe empire. The herd grazes a suburban lawn for no reason but joy. It's private and gated, so admire from the street. Proof that money can buy a Jurassic garden.

hook: Castellated 1898 prison on Cockrill Bend, closed since 1992 and a filming location for The Green Mile.
facts: The 800-cell Tennessee State Penitentiary opened on 12 February 1898, modeled on Auburn Prison in New York, with a Gothic Revival castellated main building of towers and turrets.
It sits on the Cumberland River at Cockrill Bend, near the intersection of Centennial Boulevard and Bomar Boulevard in west Nashville.
The prison was closed in 1992 following a federal court order over overcrowding and poor conditions.
After closing it served as a filming location for movies including The Green Mile (1999) and The Last Castle.
An EF3 tornado on 3 March 2020 damaged the structure, destroying roughly 40 yards of wall on one wing; the abandoned building still stands but its interior is off-limits.
The site remains closed to the public and is view-only from outside the grounds.
legend: The abandoned prison is widely reputed to be haunted, with ghost stories tied to inmates who died there.
w: A grim Gothic castle of grey stone looms over Cockrill Bend, turrets and barred windows straight from a nightmare — the old state penitentiary, opened in 1898 and shut in 1992. Hollywood filmed The Green Mile inside its decaying cellblocks. Sealed to visitors, it broods behind its walls. View the fortress of misery from the road.

hook: Restored Union stone fort built largely by African American laborers, the largest inland Civil War fort.
facts: Fort Negley was built from October to December 1862 as the largest fortification of the Union Army's occupation of Nashville and the largest inland stone fort of the Civil War.
More than 2,700 African American laborers (many self-emancipated 'contraband') built the fort, using about 62,500 cubic feet of stone; only around 300 were paid.
The fort measured roughly 600 by 300 feet, covering about four acres on St. Cloud Hill south of downtown.
Today it is a public park with a visitors center at 1100 Fort Negley Boulevard, Nashville, TN 37203.
The fort was reconstructed as a WPA project in the late 1930s and later restored as a city historic site.
w: Crowning a hill south of downtown sit the stone ramparts of the largest inland fortification of the Civil War, thrown up in 1862 by more than 2,700 African American laborers, many impressed and unpaid. Hundreds died building it. A walkway now threads the restored bastions. Hard history, with a long view over the city.

hook: Former Sunbeam bakery packed with rare and eccentric European cars, microcars and oddball prototypes.
facts: Lane Motor Museum is at 702 Murfreesboro Pike, Nashville, TN 37210, in a former Sunbeam Bakery building of about 132,000 square feet.
The Sunbeam Bakery opened on the site in 1951 and was billed as the largest and most modern bakery in the area at the time.
The collection holds roughly 150 vehicles on display and is described as the largest collection of European cars in the United States.
Most vehicles date from the 1950s through 1970s, though some are as old as the 1920s, and the focus is on rare, eccentric and microcar designs.
The museum is open to the public with regular hours and an admission fee.
w: Inside an old Sunbeam bakery sits one of the strangest car collections in America: rare European microcars, three-wheelers, propeller-driven oddities and a giant amphibious troop carrier. The museum loves the experimental and doomed over the famous. Wander the rows of automotive what-ifs. The road not taken, in metal.

hook: Elegant 1859 Italianate mansion on former plantation land between the Stones and Cumberland rivers.
facts: Two Rivers Mansion was built in 1859 in the Italianate style for David H. McGavock and his wife Willie Elizabeth Harding McGavock.
It sits at 3130 McGavock Pike, Nashville, TN 37214, on land that was part of an 1,100-acre plantation between the Stones and Cumberland rivers (the source of the name).
It is considered one of the earliest and best-preserved early Italianate houses in Middle Tennessee.
The McGavock family occupied the house for three generations until 1965.
The Metropolitan Government of Nashville and Davidson County purchased the property in 1966, and it is now a city historic site available for tours and events.
legend: The mansion is featured on local ghost tours and is said by some to be haunted by members of the McGavock family.
w: An elegant red-brick Italianate villa from 1859 stands near where the Cumberland meets the Stones river. Once the heart of a vast plantation, Two Rivers is now a city-owned event house ringed by parkland, beloved of local ghost-story tellers. Stroll the grounds for the architecture. Antebellum grandeur, faintly haunted.

hook: A single heart-shaped stone hidden among thousands of rectangular blocks in a Music Row wall.
facts: The heart is set into the stone wall around the Starstruck Entertainment building at 40 Music Square West on Music Row.
Thousands of rectangular stones form the wall, and a single one is cut in the shape of a heart.
The heart is not on the building's front; it is found on the wall along Chet Atkins Place when you turn the corner.
Starstruck Entertainment was founded by Reba McEntire and her then-husband Narvel Blackstock.
The wall and building are a fixed, publicly viewable Music Row landmark, free to find from the sidewalk.
legend: Local lore frames the lone heart as a hidden tribute to love and to having the heart to pursue creative dreams.
w: Among the thousands of rectangular blocks in a Music Row wall, one stone was cut into a perfect heart and quietly set in place — a tiny tribute to everyone chasing a dream down this street. Most walk past without spotting it. Find it on the Starstruck building and you join a small club. Love, hidden in plain sight.

hook: One of America's oldest working letterpress poster shops, hand-printing show posters since 1879 — now a working museum inside the Country Music Hall of Fame.
facts: Founded in 1879 by brothers Charles (C.R.) and Herbert (H.H.) Hatch; its first job was a handbill for the brother of Harriet Beecher Stowe, Rev. Henry Ward Beecher.

For much of the 20th century its hand-set letterpress posters were the leading advertising medium for Southern music and entertainment, including Grand Ole Opry stars such as Bill Monroe, Minnie Pearl and Ernest Tubb, plus Elvis Presley, Hank Williams and Johnny Cash.
The shop was donated to the Country Music Hall of Fame and Museum by Gaylord Entertainment in 1992.

In 2013 Hatch Show Print moved into the Country Music Hall of Fame building, now occupying spaces at 224 5th Avenue South (Rep. John Lewis Way South) comprising a print shop, retail store, classroom and the Haley Gallery.
It still designs and prints all posters by hand from its archive of vintage wood and metal type and carved blocks, and celebrated its 145th anniversary in 2024.
w: Since 1879 this letterpress shop has hand-pulled the woodblock posters that announced everyone from the Opry to Bob Dylan — one of the oldest working print shops in America. Ink-stained presses still clatter daily over a century of carved blocks. Watch a poster come off the bed. Country music, printed by hand.

hook: A grassroots museum preserving the lost music heritage of Jefferson Street — once the thriving Black R&B district where a young Jimi Hendrix cut his teeth.
facts: Located at 2004 Jefferson Street, Nashville, TN 37208, in the heart of historic North Nashville.
Founded by Lorenzo Washington, who has been conserving the area's musical legacy since around 2011.

From the 1930s/40s through the 1960s, Jefferson Street was North Nashville's Black business and entertainment hub, with clubs hosting Jimi Hendrix, Etta James, James Brown, Little Richard, B.B. King, Ray Charles, Sam Cooke and Ike & Tina Turner.
Jimi Hendrix lived and played on Jefferson Street in the early 1960s, performing at venues such as the Del Morocco and Club Baron while sharing an apartment with bandmate Billy Cox.
The museum was added to the U.S. Civil Rights Trail, recognizing Jefferson Street's role in the civil rights era.
Much of the historic district was severed in the late 1960s when Interstate 40 was routed through North Nashville, accelerating the decline of the club scene the museum commemorates.
w: A modest museum on Jefferson Street keeps the memory of a vanished music world: the Black nightclubs that once lined this strip, where Hendrix, Etta James and James Brown played in the segregation years. Founder Lorenzo Washington gathered the photos, records and stories himself. The sound that the city nearly forgot.

hook: Nashville's oldest nightlife district — a narrow downtown alley that went from publishing hub to Prohibition speakeasies to neon-lit clubs.
facts: Runs between Third and Fourth Avenues, from Union Street toward Church Street, in downtown Nashville.

Around 1915 the alley was the center of Nashville's printing and publishing trade, home to the city's two largest newspapers (The Tennessean and The Nashville Banner) plus around 13 publishers and 10 printers, giving it its name.
During Prohibition the alley became a hub of speakeasies serving illegal liquor.
From the 1940s nightclubs opened along the alley, giving stages to performers including Waylon Jennings, Dottie West, Hank Williams, Chet Atkins and Barbara Mandrell.

The 1968 legalization of liquor-by-the-drink in Tennessee restaurants helped cement Printer's Alley as a legitimate nightlife strip; current venues include the restored Skull's Rainbow Room and Bourbon Street Blues & Boogie Bar.
legend: The alley is reputed to be haunted, with the best-known tale tied to David 'Skull' Schulman, longtime owner of Skull's Rainbow Room, who was murdered there in 1998 and is said to linger in the venue.
w: A narrow downtown alley that took its name from the print shops and newspapers once crammed along it, now a glowing slot of neon, jazz and old speakeasy haunts. Through Prohibition it ran on illegal liquor; a whiff of that outlaw glamour still hangs in the brick. Duck in after dark. The city's most storied back lane.

hook: Nashville's 1902 glass-roofed shopping arcade — one of the first covered shopping centers in North America, modeled on Milan's Galleria.
facts: Opened in 1902 as Nashville's first shopping center, replacing the former Overton Alley between 4th and 5th Avenues, running between Church Street and Union Street.
Its two-story Palladian-fronted, glass-ceilinged design was inspired by Milan's Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II.
Around 40,000 people reportedly turned out for its opening, with about 40 businesses — bakeries, jewelers, fruit sellers and novelty shops — moving in.
It was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1973.
On February 13, 1960, the Arcade served as a gathering point for student protesters during the Nashville sit-ins against segregated downtown lunch counters.
Still operating today as a covered pedestrian arcade of shops, eateries and second-floor art studios.
w: A glass-roofed Victorian arcade from 1902 cuts between two downtown streets, two tiers of little shopfronts under a long skylight, a slice of old Nashville that somehow survived. Modelled on Europe's covered galleries, it now mixes lunch counters and studios. Walk through and look up at the iron and glass. Retail as it used to be.

hook: The historic Chestnut Street vinyl plant that pressed Motown's hits and the first Beatles single in America — complete with the segregation-era 'Motown Suite' upstairs.
facts: Began in 1949 as Southern Plastics, an offshoot of Nashville's Bullet Records, and moved to its longtime plant at 453 Chestnut Street in 1962 (the building erected for it in 1962).
Secured a contract to press all of Motown's singles; by the early-to-mid 1960s the plant was turning out around one million records a month.
Pressed the first Beatles 7-inch records distributed in North America (for Vee-Jay Records).

A rear apartment known as the 'United Hilton,' later the 'Motown Suite,' was built to lodge Black artists and executives — including reportedly Berry Gordy Jr., Smokey Robinson and the Supremes — who were barred from Nashville hotels under Jim Crow segregation.

It became one of the largest record pressing plants in the United States; in 2017 the company opened a larger production facility at 453 Allied Drive while the historic Chestnut Street building remained part of its legacy and tours.
A Tennessee historical marker stands near the Chestnut Street site commemorating its role in record pressing history.
w: This unassuming brick plant has been stamping out vinyl records since 1949, pressing early Motown, the Beatles' US singles and soul classics. Upstairs, the 'Motown Suite' lodged Black artists barred from segregated hotels. The big presses still run. Where the grooves were cut, and a little justice quietly done.

hook: The brick complex where Marathon Motor Works built the first cars made in the South — now a sprawling adaptive-reuse hub of lofts, shops, studios and a distillery.
facts: The site at 1305 Clinton Street began as the Nashville Cotton Mills around 1881 before Marathon Motor Works took it over.

Marathon Motor Works built automobiles here from 1909 to 1914 — billed as the first automobile manufacturer in the South — before going bankrupt; roughly 6,000–10,000 cars were made and only a handful survive.
The complex was added to the National Register of Historic Places on January 4, 1996 (ref. 95001482).
Restoration as 'Marathon Village' began after the property was acquired in 1987, becoming a model of industrial adaptive reuse.
The four-block complex today houses recording and music venues (Marathon Music Works), shops, artist studios, a radio station (Lightning 100), the Corsair Distillery and a winery.
It also hosted Antique Archaeology, the shop from the TV series 'American Pickers,' which operated there until April 2025.
w: The brick halls of the Marathon Motor Works once built whole automobiles here, in the brief years Nashville tried carmaking before 1914. Long derelict, it is now a warren of lofts, music spaces, shops and a whiskey distillery. Cobwebbed industry, given a second life. Wander the old works and breathe the machine oil.

hook: A castle-like 1900 Richardsonian Romanesque railway terminal — limestone towers, a stained-glass barrel vault — reborn as a downtown hotel.
facts: Built 1898–1900 as Nashville's Union Station, designed in Late-Victorian Romanesque Revival style by Richard Montfort (with the firm influenced by H. H. Richardson's idiom).
Constructed of Bowling Green limestone with a roughly 220-foot central clock tower; the train shed was later badly damaged.

Listed on the National Register of Historic Places on December 30, 1969, and designated a National Historic Landmark in 1975 (the landmark status was withdrawn in 2003 after fire damage to the train shed).
Rail service ceased in 1979; after standing abandoned, the terminal reopened as the Union Station Hotel in 1986.
It joined Marriott's Autograph Collection in 2012 and completed a major renovation in 2016; it is located at 1001 Broadway and is a member of Historic Hotels of America.
Original features that survive in the hotel include the soaring atrium lobby, stained-glass barrel-vaulted ceiling and ornate ironwork.
legend: A bronze statue of Mercury once crowned the tower until it was toppled by a 1952 tornado; staff lore at the hotel includes ghost stories tied to its long railway past.
w: A turreted Romanesque castle of grey stone, built in 1900 as the gateway where the railroads brought travellers to Nashville. The trains are long gone, but the barrel-vaulted lobby and stained glass survive, now a luxury hotel. A bronze Mercury once crowned it, toppled by a 1952 tornado. Arrive under a vaulted sky.

hook: A grand 1910 hotel whose real cult attraction is downstairs: a lime-green-and-black Art Deco men's room repeatedly voted the best public bathroom in America.
facts: The Hermitage Hotel, at 231 6th Avenue North, opened in 1910 as Nashville's first million-dollar, fireproof luxury hotel and is Tennessee's only AAA Five-Diamond / Forbes Five-Star hotel.
Its famous main-floor men's restroom, off the Capitol Grille, is clad in gleaming lime-green and black leaded-glass tiles with matching green fixtures and terrazzo flooring.
The Art Deco restroom interior, including the terrazzo floor, is generally dated to a 1930s redecoration and is often credited to William Caldwell, who managed the hotel in the 1940s.
It features a two-seat shoeshine station and has become a favorite backdrop for wedding and souvenir photos.
It won the Cintas 'Best Restroom in America' contest in 2008 and has topped subsequent 'most photogenic bathroom' lists; the hotel itself is on the National Register of Historic Places.
Though historically a men's room, it is widely visited by curious sightseers of all genders.
w: The grandest room in the city's oldest luxury hotel is, improbably, a men's toilet. Down a marble stair lurks a lime-green and black Art Deco restroom from 1930, all glass and chrome, so famous that women queue to peek in when it's empty. The Hermitage Hotel has hosted presidents; this is what people photograph. A loo with a fan club.

hook: Nashville's pioneering craft-cocktail speakeasy, now reopened on the fifth floor of a 1920s Gulch warehouse after 16 years on Division Street.
facts: Opened in 2009 on Division Street and is widely credited as the bar that launched Nashville's modern craft-cocktail scene.
After 16 years it closed its original 1711 Division Street space in April 2025 and reopened in May 2025.
The new location is on the fifth floor of the Bill Voorhees Building at 700 8th Avenue South in the Gulch.
The new room seats roughly 109 guests, up from about 70 at the original location, with a far more robust food menu.
It opened at the new space on Tuesday, May 13, 2025.
It is a sibling concept to Nashville's Catbird Seat and is run by the Strategic Hospitality group (the Goldberg brothers).
w: The bar that taught Nashville to take cocktails seriously, open since 2009 behind an unmarked door, its bartenders mixing with the gravity of chemists. In 2025 the Patterson House moved to a bigger fifth-floor room in the Gulch, rules and reverence intact: no standing, no phones at the bar. Order slowly, and well.

hook: East Nashville's oldest dive bar, slinging cheap beer, bourbon and a Bon Appetit-ranked cheeseburger on Gallatin Ave since 1971.
facts: Located at 411 Gallatin Ave in East Nashville (37206), in a building that has stood since the 1930s.
Established in 1971, it is considered East Nashville's oldest dive bar.
It was bought in 2015 by longtime patron Alex Wendkos, who restored elements of the original interior.
Its cheeseburger was named one of the top three burgers in the country by Bon Appetit.
Anthony Bourdain filmed at Dino's for his show 'Parts Unknown'.
The bar is open daily until 3am.
w: East Nashville's oldest dive, slinging cold beer and griddle cheeseburgers since 1971 under a neon sign on Gallatin Avenue. A new generation made it cool again, but the jukebox, the dim red light and the honest pour never changed. Late-night burgers are the move. A proper dive, holding its ground against the boom.

hook: A family-run soul-food meat-and-three that has fed Nashville for over 70 years, with recipes passed down three generations.
facts: Located at 937 Locklayer Street in Nashville (37208), on the edge of Germantown / North Nashville.
The cafe has operated for more than 70 years, with family recipes passed down three generations.
It serves breakfast and a classic meat-and-three lunch: smothered pork chops, salmon croquettes, fried chicken, greens, mac and cheese and hot-water cornbread.
Open Tuesday through Friday for breakfast and lunch (roughly 6:30am-2:30pm) plus Sunday, closed Monday and Saturday.
It is currently open (confirmed by 2026 reviews) and reachable at (615) 780-9900.
w: For seventy-odd years this tiny family kitchen has served Southern soul food to devoted regulars — fried chicken, candied yams, cornbread and a new roster of sides each day. Silver Sands is meat-and-three at its humblest and truest, all paper plates. Get there before the good stuff runs out. Grandma cooking, professionally.

hook: A neon, Blade Runner-esque robo-tiki bar in East Nashville with a giant robot hanging from the ceiling and James Beard-recognized birria tacos from the truck parked outside.
facts: Located at 1100 B Stratton Ave in East Nashville (37206), part of the neighborhood's 'tiki bar triangle'.
The room is themed around a large robot suspended from the ceiling amid tropical foliage and neon lighting.
Chopper has no kitchen; birria tacos come from Maiz de la Vida, the James Beard-semifinalist truck parked outside Tuesday through Sunday.
The drink menu features not-too-sweet tiki classics with freshly squeezed juices, house-made syrups and unusual infusions.
Seating is first-come, first-served with no reservations.
It is currently open (confirmed by 2026 reviews), closed Mondays.
w: A gleeful collision of tiki bar and sci-fi arcade, all neon, robots and rum, with a birria-taco truck out front. Chopper pours elaborate frozen cocktails under a ceiling of kitsch, with some of the best tacos in town alongside. Garish, joyful and entirely committed to the bit. Order a daiquiri and a plate of birria.

hook: A bar and restaurant set inside a converted early-1900s blacksmith shop in historic Germantown, complete with a garden courtyard and outdoor cooking stove.
facts: Located at 311 Jefferson St in Nashville's Germantown neighborhood (37208).
It occupies the John Geist and Sons Blacksmith Shop, a ca. 1900 brick building that was once Nashville's oldest business in continuous family ownership.
The blacksmith business was founded in 1886 by German immigrant John Geist Sr. and operated until 2006.
The restaurant opened in early 2018 after a renovation furnished with handcrafted pieces from local artisans.
It includes The Champagne Garden, a full garden and courtyard with an outdoor cooking stove.
It is currently open (confirmed by 2026 reviews).
w: A moody, elegant restaurant set inside a turn-of-the-century blacksmith's shop, the old forge-rooms now candlelit dining around a courtyard. Geist keeps the bones of the old smithy while plating ambitious food and serious cocktails. Atmospheric and a little hidden in Germantown. Dine where they once shod horses and beat iron.

hook: The most celebrated meat-and-three in Nashville, a cafeteria-line Southern institution that won a James Beard American Classics award.
facts: Located at 605 8th Avenue South near Lafayette Street (37203), in the Gulch / SoBro area.
It received a James Beard Foundation America's Classics award in 2009.
Founded by Jack and Rose Arnold, it is now run by their son Kahlil Arnold.
It was announced as closing in January 2023 after the family put the real estate up for sale, but that sale fell through.
Arnold's resumed regular lunch service in January 2024 and remains open under Kahlil Arnold.
It operates as a weekday lunch-only cafeteria line and is recognized in the Michelin Guide.
w: Slide your tray down the steam line and point: that's lunch at Arnold's, the cafeteria meat-and-three feeding Nashville comfort since 1982. Roast beef, fried green tomatoes, chocolate pie, ladled by the family. A James Beard classic that closed, then came roaring back in 2024. The platonic ideal of a Southern lunch.

hook: A beloved East Nashville taco spot that grew from a vintage Winnebago into a brick-and-mortar fixture famous for its fried-avocado tacos.
facts: Located at 732 McFerrin Ave in East Nashville (37206).
It began in 2008 as a taco truck out of a vintage Winnebago before settling into its current brick-and-mortar space.
Signature items include the fried-avocado taco, pulled-pork and fish tacos, elote (street corn) and weekend chilaquiles.
It also serves horchata and quart-sized margaritas on a colorful outdoor patio.
It is currently open (confirmed by 2026 reviews); it is closed Sundays.
Reachable at (615) 543-6271.
w: What began as a battered taco truck has become an East Nashville institution, beloved for handmade tortillas, fried-avocado tacos and tortilla soup ladled out of a cosy corner kitchen. Mas Tacos keeps it cash-friendly, unfussy and absurdly good. Expect a line at lunch and no reservations ever. The taco joint locals send everyone to.

hook: A 1920s-style speakeasy and cigar lounge entered through a restored red London phone booth, where you dial a secret nightly code to get in.
facts: Located at 136 Rosa L Parks Blvd in downtown Nashville (37203), steps from Broadway.
Entry is through a restored antique red London phone booth: guests dial a secret code obtained from a member, concierge or friend.
The lounge offers signature smoked Old Fashioneds with hand-cut ice, table-side absinthe fountains and 400-plus bourbons and rare spirits.
Its humidor stocks cigars including Padron, Arturo Fuente and La Flor Dominicana, sold by the stick or stored in member lockers.
It serves AVPN-certified Neapolitan pizza alongside the cocktails and cigars.
It is the Nashville outpost of the Red Phone Booth speakeasy group.
w: To get in, you find a vintage red British phone booth, dial a secret number, and a hidden door swings open onto a 1920s-style den of cocktails, cigars and low light. The Red Phone Booth leans hard into Prohibition theatre, passwords and all. Part bar, part dare. Track down the number, then step through the booth into the speakeasy.

hook: Nashville's oldest house museum, an 1799 plantation home whose interpretation now foregrounds the enslaved people who built and worked it.
facts: Built in 1799 by politician and judge John Overton (1766-1833), a close friend and advisor of Andrew Jackson and a cofounder of Memphis.
Began as a two-story, four-room Federal-style clapboard house, with later additions in 1808, 1828 and 1887.
Overton first named the property 'Golgotha' after prehistoric skulls unearthed while digging the cellar; it was later renamed Travellers Rest.
The plantation was worked for decades by enslaved Black people, whose stories the museum now centers in its interpretation.
Listed on the National Register of Historic Places since December 30, 1969.
Saved from demolition and restored in 1954 to open as a museum; located at 636 Farrell Parkway.
w: One of Nashville's oldest surviving houses, this 1799 clapboard home was built by judge John Overton over an ancient Native burial mound. Once a plantation worked by enslaved people, the museum now puts their lives at the centre of the story. Tour the rooms and the reckoning. History, told more honestly.

hook: Adelicia Acklen's opulent 1850s Italianate villa, the largest house in antebellum Tennessee, now a museum on the Belmont University campus.
facts: Built 1849-1853 in the Italian villa style as the summer home of Nashville socialite Adelicia Acklen.
With 36 rooms, it was the largest house built in Tennessee before the Civil War.
The original 175-acre estate included an art gallery, a 200-foot greenhouse and conservatory, gazebos, a bowling alley, a bath house and a zoo; the water tower and gazebos still stand.
The Belmont Mansion Association, a private nonprofit, was formed in 1972 to restore and operate the site.
Owned by the Belmont Mansion Association and Belmont University, it operates today as a historic house museum at 1900 Belmont Boulevard.
w: Adelicia Acklen, among the richest women in the antebellum South, built this Italianate villa in the 1850s and filled it with European art and marble. Twice widowed and shrewd, she once outwitted both armies to sell her cotton in the war. Now a campus museum. Gilded-age excess, with a steely mistress.

hook: The creative crossroads of East Nashville, where five streets meet amid shops, breweries, music venues and the annual Tomato Art Fest.
facts: Named for the convergence of five streets at the intersection of Woodland Street, Clearview Avenue and North 11th Street, centered in the 37206 ZIP code.
The Five Points Redevelopment District began drawing businesses in the 1970s with low-interest loans from the Metro Development and Housing Agency.
A 1998 tornado crippled the district; an early-2000s revitalization rebuilt it into a hub of shops, restaurants and live music.
A second tornado, in March 2020, again devastated Five Points and surrounding East Nashville before the area recovered.
Hosts the annual Tomato Art Fest and live-music venues including The Basement East.
w: Where streets tangle together in East Nashville, Five Points has become the neighbourhood's creative heart — murals, dive bars, record shops and music venues knotted around one quirky junction. Battered by a 2020 tornado, it came back fast. Wander out from the crossroads. East Nashville at its most itself.

hook: A cook-your-own-pancakes breakfast spot where every table has a built-in griddle and you are the chef.
facts: Each table has a griddle built into its center; diners cook their own pancakes, French toast, eggs and potatoes.
Located at 2800 Bransford Avenue, Nashville, TN 37204; phone (615) 298-2088.
Confirmed open as of June 2026, with hours Tuesday-Friday 8am-2pm, Saturday-Sunday 7am-2pm, and closed Mondays.
Offers a gluten-free-friendly batter and DIY format that makes it popular with families.
w: Breakfast here comes with a job: each table has a built-in griddle, and you pour and flip your own pancakes from a jug of batter, however you like them. The Pfunky Griddle — silent P and all — turns a meal into a game in a Berry Hill bungalow. Bring kids, or just your inner one. Make your own breakfast, badly and happily.

hook: Nashville's oldest neighborhood, an 18-block grid of restored Victorian architecture wrapped around the Nashville Farmers' Market.
facts: An 18-block district of restored Victorian buildings, it is Nashville's oldest neighborhood and its first Historic Preservation District.
Added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1979.
Architecture ranges from ornate Italianate and Queen Anne to brick cottages and shotgun houses, with cobblestone sidewalks and decorative ironwork.
The Nashville Farmers' Market sits at the neighborhood's edge (900 Rosa L. Parks Blvd) with open-air sheds, a garden center and a food hall of 30+ vendors.
The tree-lined cross streets of the core (Monroe, Adams, Jefferson) were certified as a city arboretum in 2022.
w: Nashville's oldest neighbourhood and its first historic district, a grid of restored Victorian townhouses just north of downtown. Settled by German immigrants, it faded for decades before reviving as the city's most chef-driven quarter. Gingerbread trim sits beside acclaimed restaurants. Stroll it slowly between meals.

hook: A former industrial district just south of downtown reborn as 'WeHo,' Nashville's densest cluster of art galleries, studios and maker spaces.
facts: An industrial zone of factories, mills and warehouses in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, it transformed over the past decade into one of Nashville's premier arts districts.
More than 20 galleries and studios occupy repurposed industrial buildings.
Known for the monthly first-Saturday art crawl, 'Arts & Music at Wedgewood-Houston.'
The May Hosiery Mills complex at Chestnut and Houston, once a major employer, now houses Soho House.
The Packing Plant, a former sausage-making facility, was converted into galleries, studios, a community radio station and a poetry library.
w: A gritty former industrial pocket south of downtown, WeHo has filled its old hosiery mills with galleries, studios, breweries and street murals. Once all loading docks, it now draws the creative class to first-Saturday art crawls. Raw, evolving and not yet smoothed over. Tour the murals, gallery to gallery.

hook: A West Nashville industrial neighborhood turned creative quarter, anchored by a 200-foot grain silo wearing a photorealistic mural of a local elder.
facts: Australian muralist Guido van Helten painted a roughly 160-foot photorealistic portrait on a 200-foot former Gillette Grain Company silo over about 15 days in May 2017.
The portrait depicts Lee Estes, a then-91-year-old resident who had lived in the neighborhood since the late 1920s.
An adjacent western wall depicts two children from the Saint Luke's Community House reaching upward.
The silo anchors Silo Bend, a roughly 37-acre former industrial site redeveloped as a mixed-use project named after it.
The mural is best viewed near 5202 Centennial Boulevard, close to the corner of Centennial Boulevard and 51st Avenue North.
w: A working-class West Nashville grid named for states, lately reborn with breweries, taquerias and hidden bars among the bungalows. A vast silo painted by Guido van Helten, the face of a 91-year-old local man, now watches over it all. Industrial roots, creative present. Crane your neck at the silo, then go explore.
